title_of_original: Italy and SIM 2 Multimedia SpA v Commission, Joined Cases C-328/99 and C-399/00
decided_by: Court of Justice of the European Union
case_description: [1] On 5 April 2012, the board of directors of Aerolyze SA, a French manufacturer of electric vehicle battery components and energy control systems, [2] decided to initiate liquidation proceedings due to mounting financial losses. [3] At that time, Aerolyze’s shares were held by three entities: [4] Société de Restructuration Industrielle (SRI), a company operating under French private law and wholly owned by the State, held 60 percent of the shares; [5] CapInvest SAS, a private equity firm, held 36 percent; [7] and Développement Occitanie SA, a public limited company owned by the Region of Occitanie, held the remaining 4 percent. [8] In May 2012, following expressions of concern from the French Government about the potential social and economic consequences of liquidation, [9] a recapitalisation plan was implemented involving both public and private participation. [10] According to reports later confirmed during a parliamentary hearing, [11] SRI’s decision to engage in the plan followed direct consultations with the Ministry of Economics, [12] which had urged SRI’s management to support efforts to maintain industrial capacity and employment in the region. [13] Under this plan, SRI, acting in its capacity as shareholder and creditor, [14] agreed to partially waive a debt of 90 million euros owed by Aerolyze, [15] accepting repayment of only 72 million euros and thereby forgiving 18 million euros. [16] This loss was absorbed directly by SRI from its own corporate balance sheet. [17] Développement Occitanie SA, likewise acting as a shareholder and investor, converted a previously granted loan of 6.5 million euros into equity. [18] It also provided an additional capital injection of 7.5 million euros, [19] similarly financed from its internally held resources. [20] These decisions were taken in accordance with internal governance procedures requiring prior review and approval by the Regional Council’s committee on economic affairs for investments above 5 million euros. [21] CapInvest contributed 21 million euros as new equity, and a consortium of private and public banks added 11.5 million euros in funding. [22] The public shareholders' investment was the key factor that convinced the private consortium to participate. [23] Following the implementation of the plan, the ownership structure of Aerolyze changed. [24] CapInvest became the largest shareholder with 43 percent of the capital, [25] Développement Occitanie SA held 29.5 percent, [26] the banking consortium held 22.5 percent, [27] and the remaining 5 percent was allocated to employees. [28] At the time of the recapitalisation, Aerolyze had recorded a net loss of 87 million euros for the 2011 financial year. [29] Despite a previous restructuring effort launched in 2009, the company had remained consistently unprofitable. [30] The European electric mobility sector was undergoing significant price pressure, growing international competition, and a wave of technological disruption, especially in the area of energy storage. [31] Thus, experts did not believe that the new restructuring effort could make the company profitable again.
